title: Organon: A High Cash Yield Hidden Behind Patent Fatigue
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Introduction

Organon is an American pharmaceutical company spun off from Merck & Co. in 2021 with a focus on women's health, biosimilars, and established brands. Headquartered in Jersey City, it markets a portfolio that includes contraceptives such as Nexplanon, fertility treatments, and biosimilar therapeutics designed to provide cost-effective alternatives to branded biologics. Nearly a third of the company's revenue is derived from the United States, with the balance coming from global markets where it sells products in reproductive medicine, hormone therapies, and other therapeutic categories.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 7 Warning Signs with OGN. List of 52-Week Lows List of 3-Year Lows List of 5-Year Lows Is OGN fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

In its most recent full-year results, Organon reported revenue of approximately $6.2 billion for 2025, a decline of about 3% from the prior year on both an as-reported and constant currency basis. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was roughly $1.9 billion, yielding a margin north of 30% on that measure. Management has guided 2026 revenue and EBITDA to be broadly flat with 2025, reflecting expectations for continued operational stability without meaningful top-line growth.

The fourth quarter 2025 results highlighted both the strengths and headwinds into which the company is navigating. Total quarterly revenue was down year-over-year, with the women's health segment declining and biosimilars growing modestly, driven in part by strong performance of Hadlima and contributions from newly approved products. Non-GAAP adjusted gross margins narrowed versus the prior period, and the quarter produced a net loss primarily due to a non-cash goodwill impairment associated with underperformance in select product lines.

Organon's leadership and governance recently saw a notable shift: the CEO, Kevin Ali, resigned following an internal investigation into past sales practices involving its Nexplanon implant, prompting a focus on strengthening internal controls and financial reporting procedures. Joseph Morrissey, the company's head of manufacturing and supply, was appointed interim CEO. While the board described the financial impact of those practices as small relative to overall company revenue, the incident influenced investor sentiment and share performance.

These developments frame the current context in which the company operates: a mature portfolio with solid cash generation, pressures from patent expirations and pricing, and an active management agenda focused on deleveraging and control improvements. The recent results, leadership transition, and flat guidance for 2026 together set the baseline for a valuation and owner-return analysis in the sections that follow.

Story Continues

The Economic Engine

The business is best understood as three cash-generating portfolios that behave very differently over a cycle, but all roll up into the same economic question: how much of today's cash flow is durable, and how much is slowly melting.

In 2025, Organon generated $6.216 billion of revenue. Established Brands remained the center of gravity at $3.691 billion, Women's Health contributed $1.752 billion, and Biosimilars added $691 million. The overall top line declined 3% year over year, but that headline masks a more important distinction: Women's Health and Biosimilars are the parts you can still underwrite as managed franchises, while Established Brands is the part you underwrite as an amortizing asset that must be harvested carefully.

Women's Health is not a single-product story, but it does have a single product that dominates the investor narrative. In 2025, the company described growth in its fertility business and the JADA system as meaningful offsets to declines elsewhere, but it also disclosed that Nexplanon fell 4% ex-FX for the full year. The U.S. was the weak point: Organon noted a 9% U.S. decline tied to policy-related access restrictions beginning in early 2025, partially offset by stronger performance outside the U.S. This matters because it tells you the durability of Women's Health is not purely clinical demand; it is also reimbursement and access plumbing. Organon also disclosed that JADA delivered $74 million of revenue in 2025, and then divested the JADA system on January 28, 2026. That sale reduces a small but real growth offset going forward, and it forces the rest of the portfolio to carry more of the stability burden.

Biosimilars is the only portfolio that grew in 2025, up 4% as reported and 5% ex-FX, driven primarily by Hadlima. The company also pointed to contributions from recently added or newly approved assets, including Bildyos and Bilprevda (denosumab-nxxp) and Tofidence, which it acquired in 2025. At the same time, this portfolio is not immune to the commoditizing logic of biosimilars. Organon explicitly described Renflexis and Ontruzant as being in the mature phase of their life cycles, with significant competitive pricing pressure in the U.S. and Europe. The economic takeaway is that biosimilars can be a growth bridge, but not a high-margin annuity. The underwriting question is whether the company can keep refreshing the portfolio faster than price compression erodes the older molecules.

Established Brands is where the cash comes from, and also where the decay risk lives. Revenue declined 4% as reported (5% ex-FX) in 2025. Organon pointed to contributions from Emgality and Vtama as partial offsets, while calling out declines in its respiratory portfolio and the impact from loss of exclusivity for Atozet in Europe and Japan. In the fourth quarter commentary, the company also linked respiratory weakness to pricing pressure and to guideline changes that deprioritize montelukast in several international markets, which contributed to volume declines. That is the shape of the problem here: mature products are not just subject to generic competition; they can also be structurally impaired by shifts in clinical practice and payer behavior.

Despite these cross-currents, profitability remained resilient on an adjusted basis. For 2025, Organon reported an adjusted gross margin of 60.1% and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30.7%, with adjusted EBITDA of $1.907 billion. Reported gross margin fell to 53.3% as the company cited one-time costs tied to optimizing its manufacturing and supply network, along with amortization and acquisition-related costs connected to the Dermavant acquisition completed in October 2024. The key point for an owner is that this is still a high cash-producing platform, but it is a platform whose cash flows are the result of disciplined harvesting plus selective reinvestment, not a portfolio that can simply grow its way out of leverage.

Capital Structure and Capital Allocation

Organon's operating profile reads like a cash-generative franchise set. Its capital structure reads like a leveraged harvest vehicle. As of Dec. 31, 2025, the company reported $8.64 billion of total debt and $574 million of cash and cash equivalents. With 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $1.907 billion, net leverage sits a little above 4x on management's preferred denominator. In that range, equity holders do not primarily own earnings. They own the residual after interest, taxes and the cash required to keep the portfolio from decaying faster than the balance sheet can be repaired.

What makes this credit manageable, and investable for the right kind of owner, is time. The contractual maturity schedule is front-loaded in a way that creates a single, visible wall rather than a series of near-term traps. At year-end 2025, Organon's disclosed principal maturities were roughly: $10 million in 2026, $10 million in 2027, $3.58 billion in 2028, $10 million in 2029, $21 million in 2030, and $5.07 billion thereafter. In other words, the constraint is not next quarter. It is the 2028 refinancing moment, and whether the company can walk into that window with meaningfully lower net debt and cleaner governance.

The cash engine is real, but the capital stack gets first call on it. In 2025, Organon generated $700 million of net cash provided by operating activities and spent $162 million on capital expenditures. That leaves a baseline free-cash profile that is substantial relative to the equity value the market has assigned, but not unlimited relative to the debt it has to amortize and refinance. The company's financing cash flows show how management is behaving under that constraint: 2025 included $1.513 billion of debt repayments against $1.055 billion of debt proceeds, and management cited the repurchase and cancellation of $419 million of its 2031 notes as a meaningful deleveraging action during the year. The dividend has also been intentionally reduced to a token level, the board declared a $0.02 quarterly dividend in February 2026, consistent with the company's post-2025 posture that cash belongs with debt reduction before anything else.

The cleanest capital allocation event around the turn of the year was the divestiture of the JADA system. Organon agreed to sell JADA for up to $465 million, including $440 million payable at closing (subject to customary adjustments) and a potential earn-out tied to 2026 revenue targets; the company has stated that net proceeds are intended for debt reduction, and it closed the transaction on Jan. 28, 2026. In isolation, that does not fix leverage. But it matters because it clarifies priorities: management is willing to sell a growth asset if it accelerates the balance-sheet path. For an owner underwriting a levered equity in a mixed-durability portfolio, that trade is directionally correct.

The underwriting question in this section is therefore not whether the company can generate cash in a single year. It already does. The question is whether it can keep the cash base intact long enough to shrink net debt materially before the 2028 wall. If sustainable free cash flow lands in the $600700 million neighborhood (the range implied by recent results and management's emphasis on cash durability), the math becomes straightforward: over three to four years, the business has a plausible line of sight to retire a meaningful portion of net debt even without growth, and that time value is part of the equity case. If operating erosion accelerates (Established Brands decays faster, or Women's Health faces persistent access pressure), the same maturity wall turns from time into terms, and refinancing becomes the mechanism by which value can leak out of the equityOrganon: A High Cash Yield Hidden Behind Patent Fatigue

Debt Reduction Capacity Before the Maturity Wall

The maturity schedule is only half the story. The other half is the company's ability to reduce principal before the refinancing window arrives. Organon's recent results suggest that the free cash flow base, while not immune to portfolio erosion, remains substantial relative to the size of the debt stack. In 2025, the company reported approximately $700 million of net cash provided by operating activities and spent $162 million on capital expenditures. That places normalized free cash flow comfortably in the several-hundred-million-dollar range even after interest, taxes, and maintenance investment.

If one assumes a sustainable free cash flow range of roughly $600 million to $700 million annually, a level broadly consistent with the company's recent cash generation, the arithmetic becomes straightforward. Over the three-year window leading up to the 2028 maturity wall, that implies cumulative debt reduction capacity of approximately $1.8 billion to $2.1 billion before any refinancing is required. This estimate assumes the continuation of the current capital allocation posture: a minimal dividend, limited discretionary acquisitions, and a primary focus on balance sheet repair.

That scale of potential deleveraging does not eliminate leverage risk, but it materially changes the timing of it. Entering the 2028 refinancing cycle with net debt reduced by roughly $2 billion would lower leverage ratios and reduce the amount of capital that must be rolled in a single transaction. In other words, the key asset embedded in Organon's capital structure is not growth, it is time combined with cash generation. The company does not need heroic operational improvement to create equity value; it needs the existing cash flow base to remain intact long enough for the balance sheet to shrink.

This dynamic also explains why management's recent actions have focused so heavily on debt reduction. The sale of the JADA system, completed in January 2026 for $440 million upfront with additional potential earn-out consideration, is a good example. In isolation, the transaction does not transform the capital structure. In context, it accelerates the timeline by which net debt can move meaningfully lower. For an owner evaluating a leveraged equity claim, that acceleration matters more than incremental top-line growth.

Valuation

The cleanest way to value Organon is to treat it as a capital-structure problem first and a portfolio problem second. The equity is small relative to the enterprise, so modest changes in cash flow durability or leverage do not move the debt much, they move the equity a lot.

Start with what the company actually produced in 2025 and what management is telling you to assume for 2026. Organon reported 2025 revenue of $6.216 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $1.907 billion. Management guided 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA to be broadly flat, which effectively frames the base case as hold the line rather than grow out of leverage. At year-end 2025, the business carried $8.64 billion of total debt and $574 million of cash, leaving net leverage around the low-4x range on adjusted EBITDA. That leverage is not an abstract risk factor; it is the mechanism that determines where returns accrue.

At the prevailing equity price in mid-February 2026, the market capitalization was roughly $2 billion. Add net debt and the enterprise value lands around $10 billion, which implies an EV/EBITDA multiple in the mid-5x to mid-6x range on trailing adjusted EBITDA. That is a compressed multiple for a company still producing north of $1.9 billion of adjusted EBITDA, and the market's message is straightforward: this is an amortizing portfolio with leverage, not a franchise that deserves a normal pharma multiple.

The more useful lens is the capital stack spread. In 2025, Organon disclosed cash interest expense in the mid-$400 million range, cash taxes in the high-$200 million range, and capital expenditures around $160 million. Even after one-time items, the company reported free cash flow of roughly $659 million for 2025. Put differently, the debt sits in front of an annual cash stream that is large enough to matter, and the equity sits behind it with a market value that implies a very high free-cash-flow yield. That is exactly why the stock can look optically cheap and still be risky: if the cash stream erodes, the equity absorbs the damage first; if the cash stream holds and leverage comes down, the equity captures the convexity that bondholders do not.Organon: A High Cash Yield Hidden Behind Patent Fatigue

This is the underwriting bridge I care about under a no-rerating assumption. If free cash flow can be sustained in a ~$600$700 million band and most of it is directed to debt reduction, the balance sheet can de-risk meaningfully over a three- to four-year window. Even if the enterprise multiple never improves, the equity can compound simply because the debt claim shrinks while the enterprise cash flow holds. The flip side is also true: if Established Brands decay accelerates or Women's Health erosion becomes structural, the high yield equity narrative disappears quickly because the capital structure stops relaxing.

For context, the table below shows where the market is valuing Organon versus a few large and leveraged pharma names on simple headline multiples. The point is not that Organon should trade where Pfizer or Bristol-Myers trades. The point is that the market is already imposing a distinct penalty for leverage and portfolio mix, and that makes the debate narrower: whether the current multiple already assumes enough cash-flow erosion, or whether it is over-discounting a business that still has time to de-lever.

Company TTM P/E TTM EV / EBITDA Organon & Co. 10.6x 6.4x Pfizer Inc. 20.2x 13.1x Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. 17.6x 10.8x Perrigo Company plc N/M 8.8x

Read this table as a pricing map, not a scorecard. Pfizer and Bristol-Myers trade at materially higher enterprise multiples because their balance sheets and portfolio breadth offer more margin for error. Organon's multiple sits far lower because the market is assuming that the cash engine is either shrinking or fragile, and because leverage compresses the equity's tolerance for disappointment. If you believe free cash flow is durable enough to keep chipping away at debt through the 20282031 maturities, the equity's implied cash yield is not just a statistic; it is the mechanism for owner returns. If you believe the cash stream is melting faster than leverage can fall, the low multiple is not protection, it is a warning label.

Institutional Lens

One ownership signal stands out more than the rest of Organon's shareholder register: the presence of Kahn Brothers (Trades, Portfolio) Group, a firm whose investment lineage traces directly to the earliest generation of value investing.

Kahn Brothers (Trades, Portfolio) was founded by Irving Kahn, a long-time practitioner of Benjamin Graham's investment philosophy and one of the last investors directly connected to the original Columbia value-investing tradition. The firm's approach has historically emphasized purchasing securities at a discount to intrinsic value with a clear margin of safety and a willingness to tolerate temporary volatility if the underlying asset value remains intact.

That framework helps explain why Organon appears in the portfolio. According to recent regulatory filings summarized by GuruFocus, Kahn Brothers (Trades, Portfolio) significantly increased its position in Organon, adding roughly 926,000 shares in a recent reporting period and bringing total ownership to approximately 2.49 million shares with a value of more than $37 million. The size of the increase is notable because the firm typically expands positions only when it believes market prices diverge materially from normalized business value.

Seen through that lens, Organon resembles the type of investment situation that often attracts Graham-influenced investors: a company producing meaningful cash flow but carrying a balance sheet that compresses the equity valuation. In these circumstances, the investment thesis does not require rapid growth or a dramatic strategic pivot. Instead, it rests on whether the company can preserve enough cash generation for long enough that leverage declines and the market's perception of risk moderates.

The broader shareholder base reinforces that interpretation. Quantitative and multi-strategy funds have also built positions in the stock, suggesting that Organon screens attractively on systematic factors such as low enterprise valuation and high implied free-cash-flow yield. The overlap between traditional value capital and quantitative models often appears in situations where the equity price is being driven more by balance sheet concerns than by operating performance.

Taken together, the ownership structure reflects a stock that sits at the intersection of deep value and capital-structure optionality. There is little evidence of long-duration growth investors underwriting revenue expansion. Instead, the shareholder base appears aligned around a narrower thesis: that Organon's free cash flow can remain durable long enough for leverage to decline and for equity value to emerge primarily through balance sheet repair rather than through multiple expansion.

Structural Risks

The most important risk in owning Organon is that the balance sheet magnifies any disappointment in the cash engine. Year-end net leverage was disclosed around 4.3x, which is manageable when EBITDA is stable but unforgiving if erosion accelerates. In that structure, the equity does not need a recession to get hurt; it only needs a few quarters where pricing pressure, loss of exclusivity, or access disruption pushes the cash base lower than expected. The company has also guided to a modest step down in adjusted gross margin in 2026, which signals that margin pressure is not only a historical issue but part of the near-term operating reality.

The second risk is that decline in the Established Brands portfolio is not linear. In 2025 the company quantified meaningful loss-of-exclusivity headwinds and pointed to products like Atozet as examples of how quickly revenue can be impaired once exclusivity rolls off. It also continues to describe China's volume-based procurement framework as a structural factor that can widen over time across more products. Owners should not underwrite Established Brands as a stable annuity; they should underwrite it as an amortizing asset where the slope can steepen due to policy, tender dynamics, or clinical guideline shifts.

The third risk is that the growth engine is narrower than it looks. Biosimilars grew in 2025, but management explicitly characterized older molecules such as Renflexis and Ontruzant as mature assets facing significant competitive pricing pressure, with steep declines already visible. That is the business model: portfolio refresh must outpace price compression. If launches and new assets do not replenish faster than older products decay, biosimilars becomes a treadmill rather than a stabilizer.

A fourth risk is that Women's Health durability is partly administrative. Organon attributed a meaningful U.S. Nexplanon decline in 2025 to policy-related access restrictions, which is a reminder that even clinically entrenched products can be disrupted by reimbursement and channel mechanics. The company also divested the JADA system in early 2026, removing a small but real growth offset that had been helping cushion declines elsewhere. That increases the dependence on the remaining Women's Health base to hold steady.

Finally, governance and internal controls have become a real cost of capital issue. The board disclosed the results of an internal investigation into Nexplanon wholesaler sales practices and a resulting leadership transition. Even if the direct financial impact is described as limited, it matters because the investment case depends on investor confidence in reported trends, clean comparability, and disciplined capital allocation. In a leveraged equity, credibility is not optional; it is part of the underwriting.

Conclusion

The core of the Organon thesis is not that the business will surprise on growth, but that it does not need to. At the current valuation, the equity is priced as if the cash engine is already in structural decline and the balance sheet remains a permanent constraint. That framing leaves little room for outcomes where the business simply proves more stable than feared.

What the analysis ultimately reduces to is a question of durability. If adjusted EBITDA holds in a relatively tight range and free cash flow remains anchored around the current level, the company does not need a rerating to create equity value. The path is mechanical: cash is generated, debt is reduced, and the residual claim on the business becomes incrementally more valuable over time. In that scenario, the return is driven by balance sheet compression rather than multiple expansion.

That makes this a deliberately asymmetric situation. The downside is clear, if the Established Brands portfolio erodes faster than expected or pricing pressure accelerates across biosimilars, the leverage that makes the equity interesting becomes a constraint very quickly. But if the current cash profile proves even moderately resilient, the market is already discounting that outcome more heavily than the underlying economics suggest.

On balance, the opportunity sits on the side of that asymmetry. At today's price, the market is valuing Organon as a business in decline with limited time to repair its balance sheet. The evidence points to a narrower outcome: a stable, if unexciting, cash generator with a defined path to deleveraging. If that path holds, the equity does not need to be re-rated to work it simply needs time.

title: Does This $25 Million Bet on a Stock Down 61% Signal Turnaround Potential at $6?
content:
Sio Capital Management disclosed a new position in Organon(NYSE:OGN) on February 17, 2026, acquiring 3,421,765 shares worth $24.53 million at quarter’s end.

What happened

According to a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission dated February 17, 2026, Sio Capital Management established a new position in Organon(NYSE:OGN), purchasing 3,421,765 shares. The reported position value at quarter-end increased by $24.53 million as a result of the purchase.

What else to know

This is a new position for the fund, representing roughly 4% of 13F reportable assets under management as of December 31, 2025. Top holdings after the filing:

NASDAQ:CELC: $47.58 million (7.9% of AUM) NYSE:CI: $47.40 million (7.9% of AUM) NASDAQ:SNY: $47.09 million (7.8% of AUM) NYSE:MMS: $43.19 million (7.2% of AUM) NYSE:ZBH: $34.68 million (5.7% of AUM) As of Friday, shares of Organon were priced at $6.03, down about 61% over the past year and well underperforming the S&P 500’s roughly 16% gain in the same period.

Company overview

Metric Value Revenue (TTM) $6.22 billion Net Income (TTM) $187.00 million Dividend Yield 1.3% Price (as of Friday) $6.03

Company snapshot

Organon offers a diversified portfolio of prescription therapies, including women's health products (contraception and fertility), biosimilars in immunology and oncology, cardiovascular, respiratory, dermatology, bone health, pain management, and urology treatments. It generates revenue through the development, manufacturing, and sale of branded and biosimilar pharmaceuticals, with a focus on both established and specialty therapeutic areas. The firm serves drug wholesalers, retailers, hospitals, government agencies, and managed healthcare providers across the United States and international markets.

Organon operates as a global healthcare company with a strategic emphasis on women's health and biosimilar pharmaceuticals. The company leverages a broad portfolio of established brands and specialty products to address diverse medical needs. With a strong presence in both domestic and international markets, Organon distributes its products primarily to drug wholesalers and retailers, hospitals, government agencies, and managed healthcare providers.

What this transaction means for investors

It’s been a brutal stretch for Organon, with shares down considerably over the past year and 16% since last quarter alone.

The company is not really a growth story right now. Revenue slipped 3% to about $6.2 billion last year, and profitability came under pressure, with net income falling 78% to $187 million and margins compressing as pricing and product mix weighed on results. The company is guiding for essentially flat performance in 2026, which tells you management is focused on stabilizing rather than accelerating.

Still, stability is sometimes enough when expectations are this low. The business still throws off roughly $1.9 billion in adjusted EBITDA, supported by a diversified portfolio spanning women’s health, biosimilars, and established brands. That’s cash flow that matters, especially as the company works through a heavy debt load of more than $8.5 billion.

Within a portfolio already concentrated in healthcare and defensives, this fits as a classic value tilt rather than a speculative swing, and with management being clear about its focus on maintaining operational performance and disciplined expense management this year, shares could be set for a turnaround if the company delivers.

The cancellation of this private placement does not alter the Company's exploration and investment strategy. Over the course of 2026, our exploration outlook includes up to 16 partner funded drill programs, representing more than 50,000 metres of drilling. content:
Organon (OGN) closed the most recent trading day at $6.23, moving -2.5% from the previous trading session. This move lagged the S&P 500's daily loss of 1.36%. Meanwhile, the Dow experienced a drop of 1.64%, and the technology-dominated Nasdaq saw a decrease of 1.46%.

Heading into today, shares of the pharmaceutical company had lost 12.23% over the past month, lagging the Medical sector's loss of 5.66% and the S&P 500's loss of 1.76%.

The investment community will be paying close attention to the earnings performance of Organon in its upcoming release. It is anticipated that the company will report an EPS of $0.84, marking a 17.65% fall compared to the same quarter of the previous year. For the annual period, the Zacks Consensus Estimates anticipate earnings of $3.37 per share and a revenue of $6.16 billion, signifying shifts of -7.92% and -0.86%, respectively, from the last year.

Investors might also notice recent changes to analyst estimates for Organon. Such recent modifications usually signify the changing landscape of near-term business trends. As a result, upbeat changes in estimates indicate analysts' favorable outlook on the business health and profitability.

Our research shows that these estimate changes are directly correlated with near-term stock prices. To exploit this, we've formed the Zacks Rank, a quantitative model that includes these estimate changes and presents a viable rating system.

The Zacks Rank system, running from #1 (Strong Buy) to #5 (Strong Sell), holds an admirable track record of superior performance, independently audited, with #1 stocks contributing an average annual return of +25% since 1988. Within the past 30 days, our consensus EPS projection has moved 1.72% lower. Organon is currently a Zacks Rank #5 (Strong Sell).

Looking at valuation, Organon is presently trading at a Forward P/E ratio of 1.89. This denotes a discount relative to the industry average Forward P/E of 15.34.

Meanwhile, OGN's PEG ratio is currently 0.57. The PEG ratio is akin to the commonly utilized P/E ratio, but this measure also incorporates the company's anticipated earnings growth rate. As of the close of trade yesterday, the Medical Services industry held an average PEG ratio of 1.43.

title: Investors Heavily Search Organon & Co. (OGN): Here is What You Need to Know
content:
Organon (OGN) has recently been on Zacks.com's list of the most searched stocks. Therefore, you might want to consider some of the key factors that could influence the stock's performance in the near future.

Shares of this pharmaceutical company have returned -14.2% over the past month versus the Zacks S&P 500 composite's -1.9% change. The Zacks Medical Services industry, to which Organon belongs, has lost 6.8% over this period. Now the key question is: Where could the stock be headed in the near term?

While media releases or rumors about a substantial change in a company's business prospects usually make its stock 'trending' and lead to an immediate price change, there are always some fundamental facts that eventually dominate the buy-and-hold decision-making.

Earnings Estimate Revisions

Rather than focusing on anything else, we at Zacks prioritize evaluating the change in a company's earnings projection. This is because we believe the fair value for its stock is determined by the present value of its future stream of earnings.

We essentially look at how sell-side analysts covering the stock are revising their earnings estimates to reflect the impact of the latest business trends. And if earnings estimates go up for a company, the fair value for its stock goes up. A higher fair value than the current market price drives investors' interest in buying the stock, leading to its price moving higher. This is why empirical research shows a strong correlation between trends in earnings estimate revisions and near-term stock price movements.

For the current quarter, Organon is expected to post earnings of $0.84 per share, indicating a change of -17.7% from the year-ago quarter. The Zacks Consensus Estimate has changed +11% over the last 30 days.

The consensus earnings estimate of $3.37 for the current fiscal year indicates a year-over-year change of -7.9%. This estimate has changed -1.7% over the last 30 days.

For the next fiscal year, the consensus earnings estimate of $3.64 indicates a change of +7.9% from what Organon is expected to report a year ago. Over the past month, the estimate has changed -0.8%.

Having a strong externally audited track record, our proprietary stock rating tool, the Zacks Rank, offers a more conclusive picture of a stock's price direction in the near term, since it effectively harnesses the power of earnings estimate revisions. Due to the size of the recent change in the consensus estimate, along with three other factors related to earnings estimates, Organon is rated Zacks Rank #5 (Strong Sell).

Story Continues

Projected Revenue Growth

While earnings growth is arguably the most superior indicator of a company's financial health, nothing happens as such if a business isn't able to grow its revenues. After all, it's nearly impossible for a company to increase its earnings for an extended period without increasing its revenues. So, it's important to know a company's potential revenue growth.

In the case of Organon, the consensus sales estimate of $1.46 billion for the current quarter points to a year-over-year change of -3.3%. The $6.16 billion and $6.1 billion estimates for the current and next fiscal years indicate changes of -0.9% and -1%, respectively.

Last Reported Results and Surprise History

Organon reported revenues of $1.51 billion in the last reported quarter, representing a year-over-year change of -5.3%. EPS of $0.63 for the same period compares with $0.9 a year ago.

Compared to the Zacks Consensus Estimate of $1.52 billion, the reported revenues represent a surprise of -1.08%. The EPS surprise was -13.7%.

Over the last four quarters, Organon surpassed consensus EPS estimates three times. The company topped consensus revenue estimates two times over this period.

Valuation

No investment decision can be efficient without considering a stock's valuation. Whether a stock's current price rightly reflects the intrinsic value of the underlying business and the company's growth prospects is an essential determinant of its future price performance.

While comparing the current values of a company's valuation multiples, such as price-to-earnings (P/E), price-to-sales (P/S), and price-to-cash flow (P/CF), with its own historical values helps determine whether its stock is fairly valued, overvalued, or undervalued, comparing the company relative to its peers on these parameters gives a good sense of the reasonability of the stock's price.

As part of the Zacks Style Scores system, the Zacks Value Style Score (which evaluates both traditional and unconventional valuation metrics) organizes stocks into five groups ranging from A to F (A is better than B; B is better than C; and so on), making it helpful in identifying whether a stock is overvalued, rightly valued, or temporarily undervalued.

Organon is graded A on this front, indicating that it is trading at a discount to its peers. Click here to see the values of some of the valuation metrics that have driven this grade.

Bottom Line

The facts discussed here and much other information on Zacks.com might help determine whether or not it's worthwhile paying attention to the market buzz about Organon. However, its Zacks Rank #5 does suggest that it may underperform the broader market in the near term.
